q: Diffraction is defined as the bending of waves around the corners of an obstacle into the region of geometrical shadow.
The amount of bending depends on the wavelength of light relative to the size of the aperture.
If the aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light, the bending of light will be very noticeable.
Fresnel diffraction occurs when the source of light and the screen are at a finite distance from the obstacle causing diffraction.
In Fraunhofer diffraction, there is an infinite distance between the source and the screen from the obstacle.
The diffraction pattern of a single slit consists of a bright central portion called the central maximum.
Successive bright fringes in a diffraction pattern become more intense proceeding outward from the central maximum.
Secondary wavelets from the upper and lower halves of a slit reach the center point $P_0$ in the same phase to produce a central bright maximum.
The first minimum in single-slit diffraction is formed when the path difference between waves from the center and top of the slit is $\frac{\lambda}{2}$.
For the first minimum in single-slit diffraction, the condition is $a \sin \theta = \lambda$.
Generally for the $m^{th}$ minimum in single-slit diffraction, $a \sin \theta = m \lambda$ where $m = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
The width of the central maximum in a single-slit diffraction pattern is equal to the position of the first minimum.
A narrower slit causes a wider central maximum in the diffraction pattern.
The width of the central maximum depends on the width of the slit.
Diffraction is the ___ of waves around the corners of an obstacle.
In ___ diffraction, the source and screen are at an infinite distance from the obstacle.
The bright central portion of the diffraction pattern is called the ___.
In which type of diffraction are the source and screen at a finite distance?
What happens to the intensity of successive bright fringes as you move away from the central maximum in a diffraction pattern?
What is the condition for the $m^{th}$ minimum in single-slit diffraction?
A narrower slit in a single-slit experiment results in a:
a: Central maximum of the same width

q: A very simple demonstration of light diffraction can be conducted by placing the sharp edges of a razor blade in front of intense blue light.
The amount of bending in diffraction is almost unnoticeable if the aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light.
Diffraction fringes are created by the interference of diffracted light waves.
The width of the central maximum is given by $2 \times$ position of the ___.
